channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Making and Breaking of the American Constitution,’ by Mark Peterson
item.description: In a new book, the historian Mark Peterson argues that our founding document is rooted in ideals of expansion and conquest ill suited to the nation we’ve become.
item.dc:creator: Claire Rydell Arcenas
item.category: The Making and Breaking of the American Constitution: A Thousand-Year History (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘How Flowers Made Our World,’ by David George Haskell
item.description: In “How Flowers Made Our World,” David George Haskell makes a case for their soft power.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nicolson
item.category: How Flowers Made Our World: The Story of Nature's Revolutionaries (Book)
item.media:credit: Nate Abbott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Flowering plants -- like the bloom on this puncushion cactus -- are a relatively new part of the natural world, their sudden appearance still the subject of debate.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Universal Baseball Association,’ by Robert Coover
item.description: Just in time for the start of the season, Robert Coover’s prescient 1968 novel is back in print.
item.dc:creator: Adam Dalva
item.category: Universal Baseball Association, Inc, J Henry Waugh, Prop (Book)
item.media:description: Robert Coover’s novel “The Universal Baseball Association” long preceded the rise of fantasy sports but captured their distorting effects.

item.title: The 10 Best Books of 2025
item.description: The staff of The New York Times Book Review choose the year’s top fiction and nonfiction.
item.category: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny (Book)
There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America (Book)
Mother Emanuel: Two Centuries of Race, Resistance, and Forgiveness in One Charleston Church (Book)
A Marriage at Sea: A True Story of Love, Obsession, and Shipwreck (Book)
Wild Thing: A Life of Paul Gauguin (Book)

item.title: Han Kang Among National Book Critics Circle Award Winners
item.description: This year’s winners include the latest novel by the South Korean Nobel laureate in literature and a memoir by one of India’s best known novelists.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: From left: Mayank Austen Soof; Pascal Le Segretain/Getty Images; Maciek Jasik
item.media:description: Arundhati Roy, Han Kang and Kevin Young.

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: Playmakers: The Jewish Entrepreneurs Who Created the Toy Industry in America (Book)
Darkology: Blackface and the American Way of Entertainment (Book)
How Flowers Made Our World: The Story of Nature's Revolutionaries (Book)

item.title: Carole Radziwill on FX’s ‘Love Story’ and Returning to ‘Real Housewives’
item.description: Her deceased loved ones are characters on a hit TV show, her name is in the Epstein files and she’s returning to “Real Housewives.” What does she make of it all?
item.dc:creator: Iva Dixit
item.category: Love Story: John F. Kennedy Jr. & Carolyn Bessette (TV Program)
The Real Housewives of New York (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Lucia Bell-Epstein for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rebutting others’ accounts of her deceased loved ones — her husband, Anthony Radziwill; his cousin, John F. Kennedy, Jr.; and Carolyn Bessette Kennedy — has long been something of a duty for Carole Radziwill. But even as she has readily taken up that cause, she is also tired of doing it.

item.title: 8 Thriller Books About Housewives Getting Revenge
item.description: The author Elizabeth Arnott recommends thrilling tales of domestic vengeance and feminine power.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Arnott

item.title: A Free Home for San Francisco Artists, From Dave Eggers and Friends
item.description: The writer, and the artist JD Beltran, have come up with Art + Water, to host exhibitions, give 30 artists studio space, and offer community events.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Poppy Lynch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The view of the bay from Pier 29, which will house Art + Water, a teaching and community organization. The first show will be devoted to the Bay Area filmmaker Boots Riley, and curated by an Art + Water co-director, René de Guzman.

item.title: Tracy Kidder, Author of ‘The Soul of a New Machine,’ Dies at 80
item.description: A Pulitzer Prize-winning narrative journalist, he wrote deeply reported books that often focused on heroic goodness in people.
item.dc:creator: John Schwartz
item.category: Soul of a New Machine, The (Book)
Mountains Beyond Mountains: The Quest of Dr. Paul Farmer, a Man Who Would Cure the World (Book)
Rough Sleepers: Dr. Jim O'Connell's Urgent Mission to Bring Healing to Homeless People (Book)
item.media:credit: Fred R. Conrad/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tracy Kidder in 1999 in Northampton, Mass. He moved to rural Western Massachusetts after completing a creative writing master’s program at the University of Iowa.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Insatiable Machine,’ by Trevor Jackson
item.description: A new history by Trevor Jackson argues that the economic system that transformed global living standards depends on endless growth impossible to sustain.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: The Insatiable Machine: How Capitalism Conquered the World (Book)
item.media:description: Capitalism, as Trevor Jackson explains it, is reflexively voracious: “It obeys the dumb, inhuman logic of its own unthinking operation. It cannot stop growing or expanding.”

item.title: Book Review: ‘American Men,’ by Jordan Ritter Conn; ‘Who Needs Friends,’ by Andrew McCarthy
item.description: “American Men,” by Jordan Ritter Conn, and “Who Needs Friends,” by Andrew McCarthy, report from the front lines of the epidemic of male loneliness.
item.dc:creator: Mark Oppenheimer
item.category: Who Needs Friends: An Unscientific Examination of Male Friendship Across America (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Treacherous Secret Agent,’ by Marjorie Garber
item.description: In a new book, the Harvard scholar Marjorie Garber suggests how Americans targeted during the Red Scare used literature to confound their interrogators.
item.dc:creator: A.O. Scott
item.category: A Treacherous Secret Agent: How Literature Spoke Truth to Power During the Red Scare (Book)
item.media:description: The theater producer Joseph Papp, right, at a hearing before the House Un-American Activities Committee in 1958.

item.title: ‘Lonesome Dove,’ ‘Brokeback Mountain’ and the Power of the Book Review in the Age Before Algorithms
item.description: How The Washington Post’s now-defunct Book World transformed the careers of two giants of American literature.
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld

item.title: Book Review: ‘Open Space,’ by David Ariosto
item.description: “Open Space,” by David Ariosto, suggests there are few limits on human ingenuity that could prevent us from colonizing the cosmos.
item.dc:creator: Adam Becker
item.category: Open Space: From Earth to Eternity -- the Global Race to Explore and Conquer the Cosmos (Book)
item.media:credit: Brandon Thibodeaux for The New York Times

item.title: John Lithgow as Roald Dahl in ‘Giant’: A Study in Monstrosity
item.description: In Mark Rosenblatt’s play, a powerful portrayal of the beloved children’s book author who almost gleefully exposes his bigotry.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: John Lithgow in “Giant” at the Music Box Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Brian Doherty, 57, Dies; Chronicled Libertarians and Other Outsiders
item.description: Fascinated by the fringes, he wrote a definitive history of libertarianism and books about underground comics and the Burning Man festival.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Book Review: ‘Almost Life,’ by Kiran Millwood Hargrave
item.description: In Kiran Millwood Hargrave’s novel “Almost Life,” a passionate love affair between two college women gives way to a lifetime of what-ifs.
item.dc:creator: Stacey D’Erasmo

item.title: What’s It Like to Be Back in Print After 20 Years? A Bit Odd.
item.description: Nancy Lemann published her first novel at 28. Then came “the doom.” Now she’s back in the spotlight, and not exactly comfortable with it.
item.dc:creator: Marie Solis
item.media:credit: Annie Flanagan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The author outside the apartment in New Orleans where she wrote “Lives of the Saints.”

item.title: Book Review: ‘Darkology,’ by Rhae Lynn Barnes
item.description: A new book by Rhae Lynn Barnes examines how minstrelsy once occupied the center of the nation’s cultural life.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.category: Darkology: Blackface and the American Way of Entertainment (Book)
item.media:credit: Dorothea Lange, via Library of Congress
item.media:description: Performers at a federal migratory labor camp in 1938.

item.title: Sparkling, Stunning New Romance Books
item.description: Our columnist on three sparkling new romances.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Waite

item.title: Book Review: ‘Playmakers: The Jewish Entrepreneurs Who Created the Toy Industry in America,’ by Michael Kimmel
item.description: In “Playmakers,” Michael Kimmel traces, and celebrates, the immigrant roots of the American toy industry. (Batteries not included.)
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: Playmakers: The Jewish Entrepreneurs Who Created the Toy Industry in America (Book)

item.title: Who Is Antigone? The 2500-Year-Old Rebel With a Cause.
item.description: “Antigone” gave us the original “bad girl,” but its themes go beyond that. How do adaptations keep making Sophocles’ ideas about democracy and theater new?
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Book Review: ‘Everybody’s Fly,’ by Fab 5 Freddy
item.description: As his new memoir demonstrates, he himself would achieve fame as a visual artist, filmmaker, TV host and formative tastemaker.
item.dc:creator: Alan Light
item.category: Everybody's Fly: A Life of Art, Music, and Changing the Culture (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Feather Wars,’ by James H. McCommons
item.description: In “The Feather Wars,” James H. McCommons pays tribute to the nation’s first conservationists.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Hammer
item.category: The Feather Wars: And the Great Crusade to Save America's Birds (Book)
item.media:description: The Delineator, a creation of the Butterick Publishing Company, set fashion trends and promoted its sewing patterns to home seamstresses. Here, a page highlights the Victorian craze for elaborate plumage, one of several fads to threaten bird species at the time.

item.title: The Month’s Best New Mystery Books
item.description: Our critic on three terrific new mysteries and a gem-filled story collection.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Weinman
item.category: The Tree of Light and Flowers (Book)

item.title: Don DeLillo’s Hockey Novel, ‘Amazons,’ Will Return to Stores
item.description: You’re welcome.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: ‘Monte Cristo’ Review: A Tale of Betrayal, on Two Counts
item.description: An adaptation has a twist that doesn’t track, and songs that benefit from an excellent cast, including Norm Lewis, Sierra Boggess and Adam Jacobs.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: What the ‘Project Hail Mary’ Directors Learned From a Firing
item.description: Phil Lord and Christopher Miller, best known for animations like the “Spider-Verse” films, took lessons from “Solo: A Star Wars Story,” a project from which they were dismissed.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Lila Barth for The New York Times
item.media:description: Phil Lord, left, and Christopher Miller. “It was almost contrarian to make a movie that’s so affirming about a topic that is on everyone’s mind, which is this idea that we’re doomed,” Lord said.

item.title: 10 Kids’ Books for Fans of The Baby-Sitters Club
item.description: Ten recommendations for fans of Ann M. Martin’s iconic paperback series.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Hubert Swan
item.category: A Babysitters Guide to Monster Hunting (Book)
